worth_it_explanation: Unified Pricing Rules (UPR) set the floor every piece of programmatic demand has to clear in Google Ad Manager, so they are the single largest control a publisher has over programmatic revenue. The audit reads directly from the pricing rules screen, so it is quick to run and it surfaces money left on the table. A floor set too low lets demand clear cheaply, and a floor set without thought to the inventory's value either suppresses fill or undersells it. The fix is a configuration change in the account, not a rebuild, and it takes effect on the next auction.
intro: Unified Pricing Rules centralize the floor prices that programmatic demand has to clear in Google Ad Manager, covering Open Auction, Private Auctions, First Look, and Open Bidding. The floor decides how much of the inventory's value the publisher keeps rather than handing to the buyer, so it is the lever with the most direct effect on programmatic revenue. These checks confirm that monetized inventory carries intentional floors, that rules do not collide in ways that produce unintended prices, and that branded and semi-transparent inventory are priced for their different value to buyers.

audit_checks.title: Inventory runs under the default Unified Pricing Rule with no custom floors
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When inventory clears under the default rule, the floor is very low or zero, so programmatic demand wins at the bottom of what it would have paid. The publisher captures a fraction of the inventory's value, and because the rule reads as present the shortfall does not announce itself.

educate.base:
Unified Pricing Rules (UPR) set the auction floor that non-guaranteed programmatic demand has to clear in Google Ad Manager, across Open Auction, Private Auctions, First Look, and Open Bidding. Every Ad Manager network has a default rule that applies wherever no more specific rule targets the inventory. That default carries a very low or zero floor, so inventory that runs under it lets demand clear at the bottom of what it would have paid.

The floor is the share of the inventory's value the publisher keeps rather than ceding to the buyer. When the default rule governs monetized inventory, bids that would have cleared a thoughtful floor instead clear at or near zero, and the revenue difference is the gap between what the inventory is worth and what it was allowed to charge. The account still looks configured because a rule exists. The problem is which rule, not whether one is present.

The fix is to create rules scoped to the inventory they should price, by ad unit, placement, geography, format, or buyer, rather than leaving valuable inventory under the catch-all default. Google Ad Manager also offers optimized floor prices as a pricing option inside a unified pricing rule, which sets the floor per query rather than holding a fixed number, for publishers who would rather have the floor adjust than maintain it by hand.

A few signs point to inventory running under the default with no intentional floor:
  • The pricing rules screen shows only the default rule, or custom rules that target a narrow slice while the rest of the inventory falls through to the default
  • The default rule carries a floor of zero or a token amount
  • High-value placements share the same near-zero floor as remnant inventory
You may want to confirm which rule actually governs each monetized placement before concluding the floors are intentional. A rule existing in the account does not mean it reaches the inventory that needs it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, the inventory's value varies sharply by section and placement, so a single default floor underprices the strong placements while it does little for weak ones. You may want to scope rules to the high-value placements first, for example an above-the-fold unit on a flagship section, rather than relying on one floor across everything.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages often draw higher-intent traffic that buyers value, so leaving those pages under the default floor can undersell the inventory most worth protecting. You may want to set rules that price the commercial pages for what that audience is worth to demand.

learn_more.source_title: Optimize floor prices in unified pricing rules (Beta)

investigate.intro: Confirm whether monetized inventory is covered by intentional custom rules or falls through to the default rule, and read the floor each governing rule carries.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'Pricing rules', to see the unified pricing rules in the network.
Note whether custom rules exist beyond the default rule, and read the default rule's floor price. A zero or token default means inventory that no custom rule reaches clears at the bottom.
For each custom rule, read its targeting and record which inventory it covers. Build a short list of the monetized ad units or placements that no custom rule targets.
For a sample of high-value placements, trace which rule applies and confirm it is an intentional rule rather than the default.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per monetized placement sampled, which pricing rule governs it and the floor that rule carries. A placement covered by an intentional custom rule with a deliberate floor passes. A placement that falls through to the default rule, or sits under a custom rule with a zero or token floor that was not chosen for that inventory, is a fail recorded with the placement name and the floor it currently clears at.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the governing rule and floor for each monetized placement above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that monetized inventory on {{site_name}} runs under the default Unified Pricing Rule (UPR) with no custom floor. The default carries a very low or zero floor, so programmatic demand clears at the bottom of what it would have paid, and {{site_name}} keeps a fraction of the inventory's value. {{voice_recommend}} creating pricing rules scoped to the inventory they should price, by ad unit, placement, geography, or buyer, so that valuable placements carry deliberate floors rather than falling through to the default. For inventory where a fixed floor is hard to maintain, {{voice_recommend}} testing optimized floor prices, which set the floor per query inside a unified pricing rule. After the change, confirm on the pricing rules screen that each monetized placement is governed by an intentional rule.

audit_checks.title: Unified Pricing Rules overlap or conflict
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When more than one rule targets the same inventory, Google Ad Manager applies the rule with the higher price, so overlapping rules can hand inventory a floor the publisher never meant it to have. The price that governs the auction is then an accident of targeting rather than a decision, which suppresses fill or undersells depending on which rule wins.

educate.base:
Unified Pricing Rules (UPR) target inventory through dimensions such as ad unit, placement, geography, device, format, and buyer. When two rules target the same inventory, they overlap, and Google Ad Manager resolves the conflict by applying the rule with the higher price. The floor that governs the auction is then decided by which rule happens to be more expensive, rather than by which rule the publisher intended for that inventory.

An overlap is easy to create without noticing. A broad rule that prices a whole ad unit and a narrower rule that prices one geography inside it both target the same impressions, and the higher of the two wins on every impression they share. If the broad rule is the higher one, the narrow rule never takes effect. If the narrow rule is higher, it lifts the floor on impressions the publisher may have wanted priced lower for fill. Either way the result is a floor that was not chosen on purpose.

The way to keep this clean is to scope rule targeting so that rules meant for different inventory do not collide, and to confirm which rule actually governs a sample of inventory rather than assuming the most specific rule wins. Google Ad Manager offers an overlap exploration view in pricing rules that shows which rules apply together on a given inventory segment, which helps trace a floor back to the rule that set it.

A few signs point to rules overlapping in ways that were not intended:
  • Several rules target the same ad unit or placement through different dimensions
  • A floor on a placement does not match the rule the practitioner expected to govern it
  • A narrow rule appears to have no effect because a broader, higher-priced rule covers the same impressions
You may want to trace which rule wins on a sample of inventory before treating the floors as settled, given that the governing rule is the higher-priced one, not the most specific one.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site with rules layered by section, geography, and format, overlaps accumulate as rules are added over time, and an old broad rule can outprice a newer targeted one without showing it on the screen. You may want to use the overlap exploration view to confirm which rule governs a flagship placement rather than assuming the newest or narrowest rule applies.

investigate.intro: Review the targeting of the unified pricing rules for overlap, then trace which rule governs a sample of inventory to confirm the winning floor is intentional.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'Pricing rules', and list the active unified pricing rules with their targeting dimensions.
Identify rules whose targeting overlaps, for example a rule on a whole ad unit and a rule on one geography or format inside that ad unit.
Open the overlap exploration view for a sample inventory segment and read which rules apply together and which floor wins.
For each overlap, record whether the higher-priced rule that governs the inventory is the one the publisher intended, or an accident of two rules colliding.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per sampled inventory segment with overlapping rules, which rule wins and whether that winning floor was intended. A segment where the governing rule is the one the publisher meant to apply, or where overlap is intentional and the precedence is understood, passes. A segment where a rule collision hands the inventory an unintended floor is a fail recorded with the segment, the rules that overlap, and the floor that currently governs it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the overlapping rules and the winning floor for each sampled inventory segment above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that Unified Pricing Rules (UPR) on {{site_name}} overlap in ways that produce unintended floors. When more than one rule targets the same inventory, Google Ad Manager applies the higher-priced rule, so the floor governing the auction is decided by which rule is more expensive rather than by which rule was meant for that inventory. {{voice_recommend}} scoping rule targeting so that rules intended for different inventory do not collide, and tracing the winning rule on the affected segments using the overlap exploration view. After the change, confirm that the rule governing each sampled segment is the one intended for it.

audit_checks.title: Branded and anonymous inventory are priced the same
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Branded inventory, where the buyer sees the full URL, is worth more to demand than semi-transparent inventory, where the buyer sees only the domain. Pricing both at one floor either underprices the branded inventory buyers would pay more for or sets the semi-transparent floor too high for the demand it attracts.

educate.base:
In Google Ad Manager, a publisher labels the URLs it represents as either branded or semi-transparent. On branded inventory the buyer sees the full URL in the bid request, so the buyer knows exactly where the ad will run. On semi-transparent inventory, which is sometimes called anonymous, the buyer sees only the domain rather than the subdomain or path, so the placement is partly hidden. Buyers pay more when they can see where their ad runs, so branded inventory carries more value to demand than the same impression sold semi-transparently.

Unified Pricing Rules (UPR) can set different floors for branded and semi-transparent inventory by targeting the inventory URLs the publisher has labeled. When a single floor prices both the same, one of two things happens. Either the branded inventory carries the lower semi-transparent floor and undersells demand that would have paid more for the visible placement, or the semi-transparent inventory carries the higher branded floor and suppresses fill on demand that values the hidden placement less.

The way to price for the difference is to label inventory URLs as branded or semi-transparent under 'Inventory', then 'URLs', and to write pricing rules that target those URL values so each inventory type carries a floor that reflects what it is worth to buyers. Mobile app inventory always transacts as branded, so the semi-transparent setting applies to desktop and mobile web.

You may want to confirm whether the pricing rules distinguish branded from semi-transparent inventory at all, and if they do, whether the two floors reflect the different value buyers place on a visible versus a hidden placement.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site that sells some inventory semi-transparently to protect a premium brand, the branded and semi-transparent floors should reflect how much the visible URL is worth to buyers. You may want to confirm the branded placements carry a higher floor than the same inventory sold semi-transparently, rather than a single floor across both.

investigate.intro: Check whether inventory URLs are labeled branded or semi-transparent, and whether the pricing rules set floors that reflect the different value of each inventory type.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'URLs', and read how each represented URL is labeled, branded or semi-transparent.
Open 'Inventory', then 'Pricing rules', and find the rules that target those URL values.
Record whether any rule differentiates branded from semi-transparent inventory, and read the floor each carries.
Where branded and semi-transparent inventory share a single floor, note the URL values affected so the finding names the inventory priced without regard to type.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per inventory URL or group sampled, whether it is labeled branded or semi-transparent and the floor the governing rule sets. Inventory whose branded and semi-transparent floors reflect the different value buyers place on each passes. Inventory where branded and semi-transparent impressions clear the same floor, with no rule distinguishing the two, is a fail recorded with the URL values and the shared floor.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the branded or semi-transparent label and the governing floor for each inventory URL above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that branded and semi-transparent inventory on {{site_name}} clear the same floor in Unified Pricing Rules (UPR). Branded inventory shows the buyer the full URL and is worth more to demand than semi-transparent inventory, which shows only the domain, so a single floor either undersells the branded placements or sets the semi-transparent floor too high for the demand it attracts. {{voice_recommend}} labeling inventory URLs as branded or semi-transparent and writing pricing rules that target those URL values, so each inventory type carries a floor that reflects what it is worth to buyers. After the change, confirm that branded placements carry a higher floor than the same inventory sold semi-transparently.
